nodes.title: Define an antiderivative (primitive)
nodes.brief: Recognise an antiderivative of f as a function whose derivative is f.
nodes.quote: The functions that could possibly have given function as a derivative are called anti derivatives (or primitive) of the function.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — F is an antiderivative of f if F'(x) = f(x).
An antiderivative (or primitive) of f is any function F whose derivative is f. To find one, ask 'what function differentiates to f?'. Example: an antiderivative of 2x is x^2, because d/dx(x^2) = 2x.

PART 2 — Antiderivatives are not unique: add + C.
Since the derivative of any constant is 0, if F is an antiderivative then so is F + C for every constant C. So x^2, x^2 + 1, x^2 - 7 are all antiderivatives of 2x. Always include the arbitrary constant C; the antiderivative is a whole family, not a single function.
keyMoves: Ask which function differentiates to the given f
Recognise F is an antiderivative when F'(x) = f(x)
Add an arbitrary constant C, since it differentiates to 0
misconceptions: Thinking the antiderivative is unique (forgetting + C)
Confusing the antiderivative with the derivative

refreshers.trigger: Part 2 justifies the arbitrary constant + C on the fact that a constant differentiates to 0; a student who does not recall the Class 11 rule that d/dx(constant) = 0 cannot see why F + C is also an antiderivative of f.
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: quick one before we add that + C: what is the derivative of a plain constant, like d/dx(7)?
ladder: The derivative measures the rate of change (the slope) of a function as x moves.
Picture the graph of y = 7: it is a flat horizontal line, the same height for every x.
A flat line has no rise as you move along it, so its slope is 0 everywhere.
So the derivative of any constant is 0: d/dx(7) = 0, d/dx(-3) = 0, d/dx(C) = 0 for every constant C.
refreshers.answer: The derivative of any constant is 0, because a constant function is a flat horizontal line with zero slope everywhere: d/dx(7) = 0, and in general d/dx(C) = 0 for any constant C. That is exactly why adding a constant to an antiderivative changes nothing when you differentiate: if F'(x) = f(x), then (F + C)'(x) = F'(x) + 0 = f(x) too. So x^2, x^2 + 1, and x^2 - 7 are all antiderivatives of 2x — the + C just rides along, contributing 0 to the derivative.
refreshers.returnCue: so since every constant differentiates to 0, F and F + C have the same derivative f — that is why we tack on the arbitrary constant C and call the antiderivative a whole family, not one function.

nodes.title: Recognise integration as the inverse of differentiation
nodes.brief: Understand integration (antidifferentiation) as the reverse of differentiation.
nodes.quote: Integration is the inverse process of differentiation.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Integration reverses differentiation.
Given a derivative, integration recovers the original function (the antiderivative). It is the inverse operation, also called antidifferentiation. So every standard derivative, read backwards, is a standard integral: since d/dx(sin x) = cos x, the integral of cos x is sin x + C.

PART 2 — Every differentiation rule has an integration counterpart.
The chain rule reversed becomes substitution; the product rule reversed becomes integration by parts. When reversing, never forget the constant of integration C — it is the one piece differentiation discards.
keyMoves: Read each derivative rule backwards to get an integral
Recover the original function from its derivative
Treat integration and differentiation as inverse operations
misconceptions: Believing integration is unrelated to differentiation
Forgetting the constant of integration when reversing

nodes.title: Interpret the indefinite integral symbol
nodes.brief: Read the integral sign of f dx as the whole family of antiderivatives F(x) + C.
nodes.quote: which will represent the entire class of anti derivatives read as the indefinite integral of *f* with respect to *x*.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The integral sign denotes the WHOLE family of antiderivatives.
The symbol integral of f(x) dx means 'all antiderivatives of f', read as the indefinite integral of f with respect to x. It equals F(x) + C, where F is any one antiderivative and C is the arbitrary constant. So it is a family of functions, not a single number.

PART 2 — Always write + C.
The indefinite integral is NOT a number (that is the definite integral). It is a set of functions, so the answer must carry the constant of integration C. Dropping C gives only one member of the family, which is incomplete.
keyMoves: Read the integral of f dx as 'all antiderivatives of f'
Write the answer as F(x) + C
Always include the constant of integration C
misconceptions: Dropping the constant of integration C
Treating the indefinite integral as a single number

nodes.title: Use that equal derivatives give the same integral family
nodes.brief: Two indefinite integrals with the same derivative differ only by a constant.
nodes.quote: Two indefinite integrals with the same derivative lead to the same family of curves and so they are equivalent.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Same derivative means same family (differ only by a constant).
If two functions have the same derivative, they differ only by a constant. So two indefinite integrals of the same function are equivalent — they represent the same family of curves. This is why the same integral can appear in different-looking forms that differ by a constant.

PART 2 — Verify answers by differentiating; use linearity.
To check an integral, differentiate it: if you recover the integrand, it is correct (any constant difference is fine). This also justifies linearity: the integral of (f + g) is the sum of the integrals, and constant multipliers factor out of the integral sign.
keyMoves: Compare two answers by differentiating them
If their derivatives match, the answers differ only by a constant
Use linearity: split sums and pull out constant multipliers
misconceptions: Thinking two correct integrals must look identical
Treating differently-constant answers as contradictory

nodes.title: Integrate by substitution (change of variable)
nodes.brief: Substitute a new variable whose derivative occurs in the integrand to simplify the integral.
nodes.quote: This change of variable formula is one of the important tools available to us in the name of integration by substitution.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Substitute t for an inner function whose derivative also appears.
Integration by substitution reverses the chain rule. Choose t = (an inner function) whose derivative ALSO appears in the integrand, so that dt absorbs it. Example: for the integral of 2x*(x^2+1) dx, put t = x^2 + 1, then dt = 2x dx.

PART 2 — Integrate in t, then substitute back to x.
Rewrite the whole integral in terms of t (the integral above becomes the integral of t dt = t^2/2 + C), integrate, then replace t with its expression in x: (x^2+1)^2/2 + C. The two common errors are not converting dx into dt and forgetting to substitute back at the end.
keyMoves: Spot a function whose derivative also appears in the integrand
Set t equal to that function; replace its derivative dx by dt
Integrate in t, then substitute back to x
misconceptions: Forgetting to convert dx into dt (or vice versa)
Not substituting the original variable back at the end

nodes.title: Classify a rational function as proper or improper
nodes.brief: Call a rational function proper if degree of numerator < degree of denominator, otherwise improper.
nodes.quote: then the rational function is called proper, otherwise, it is called improper.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Compare the DEGREES of numerator and denominator.
A rational function is P(x)/Q(x) with Q(x) != 0. It is PROPER if deg(P) < deg(Q), and IMPROPER if deg(P) >= deg(Q). Compare degrees (highest powers), not leading coefficients. Example: (x+1)/(x^2-4) is proper (1 < 2); (x^2)/(x-1) is improper (2 >= 1).

PART 2 — Reduce an improper one by division first.
Before applying partial fractions, an improper rational function must be reduced by polynomial long division to a polynomial PLUS a proper rational function. Applying partial fractions directly to an improper fraction (without dividing first) is the common error.
keyMoves: Compare the degree of the numerator with that of the denominator
Proper if numerator degree is smaller; improper otherwise
Reduce an improper one by division before applying partial fractions
misconceptions: Comparing leading coefficients instead of degrees
Applying partial fractions to an improper fraction without dividing first

refreshers.answer: The degree of 3x^2 - 5x + 7 is 2, because the highest power of x present is x^2. Degree counts the highest exponent of x, not the size of any coefficient. (Examples: x^2/(x-1) has numerator degree 2 and denominator degree 1, so it is improper; (x+1)/(x^2-4) has numerator degree 1 < denominator degree 2, so it is proper.)
ladder: A polynomial is a sum of terms, each a constant times a power of x: e.g. 7 (= 7x^0), -5x (= -5x^1), 3x^2.
The 'degree' of a polynomial is the highest power of x that appears with a non-zero coefficient.
So in 3x^2 - 5x + 7, the powers present are 2, 1, and 0. The highest is 2.
Watch the leading coefficient is irrelevant to the degree: 100x + 1 has degree 1, while x^2 has degree 2, even though 100 > 1.
refreshers.trigger: Student must compare the degree of the numerator with the degree of the denominator to classify a rational function as proper or improper, but may not recall that 'degree' means the highest power of x.
refreshers.returnCue: Now that 'degree' = highest power of x, compare deg(numerator) with deg(denominator): proper if numerator's is smaller, improper otherwise.
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: In the polynomial 3x^2 - 5x + 7, what is its 'degree'?

refreshers.trigger: Reducing an improper rational function requires polynomial long division — dividing P(x) by Q(x) to get a quotient plus a remainder over Q(x) — but the student may not recall how to actually carry out the division of one polynomial by another from Class 10.
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: quick one before we divide: when you split 17 by 5, you get '3 with 2 left over' — can we do that same kind of dividing with polynomials?
ladder: Remember plain number division: 17 / 5 = 3 remainder 2, which we can write as 17 = 5 x 3 + 2, or 17/5 = 3 + 2/5. The remainder (2) is always smaller than the divisor (5).
Polynomials work the same way. To divide x^2 by (x - 1), ask: what do I multiply (x - 1) by to match the highest term x^2? Answer: x, since x times (x - 1) = x^2 - x.

Subtract that from x^2: x^2 - (x^2 - x) = x. Now the leftover x still has degree 1, same as the divisor, so we keep going: what times (x - 1) gives x? Answer: 1, since 1 times (x - 1) = x - 1. Subtract: x - (x - 1) = 1.
Now the leftover is just 1 (degree 0, smaller than the divisor's degree 1), so we stop. Quotient = x + 1, remainder = 1. So x^2/(x - 1) = (x + 1) + 1/(x - 1).
refreshers.answer: Dividing one polynomial by another is just like long division of numbers: at each step you match the highest power, multiply the divisor, subtract, and bring down what's left, stopping once the remainder's degree is smaller than the divisor's. The result is P(x)/Q(x) = quotient(x) + remainder(x)/Q(x), where the remainder piece is now a PROPER fraction. That last piece is exactly the reduction this node asks for: before partial fractions, an improper rational function gets turned by this division into a polynomial PLUS a proper rational function.
refreshers.returnCue: With the division done, you've split the improper fraction into 'polynomial + proper fraction' — set the polynomial aside and carry only the leftover proper piece on to partial fractions.

nodes.title: Integrate by partial fraction decomposition
nodes.brief: Split a proper rational function into simpler fractions, then integrate each piece.
nodes.quote: It is always possible to write the integrand as a sum of simpler rational functions by a method called partial fraction decomposition.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Decompose a proper rational function into simpler fractions.
A proper rational function can be written as a sum of simpler fractions. Factor the denominator, then write one partial fraction per factor using the standard forms: a linear factor (x-a) gives A/(x-a); a repeated factor (x-a)^2 gives A/(x-a) + B/(x-a)^2; an irreducible quadratic gives (Ax+B)/(quadratic). Solve for the unknown constants.

PART 2 — Integrate each simple fraction.
Each piece integrates easily: A/(x-a) gives A log|x-a|, and quadratic pieces give arctan/log terms. Example: 1/((x-1)(x-2)) = -1/(x-1) + 1/(x-2), integrating to log|x-2| - log|x-1| + C. Confirm the fraction is proper first (divide if not).
keyMoves: Confirm the rational function is proper (reduce first if not)
Write it as a sum of standard partial fractions and solve for the constants
Integrate each simple fraction separately
misconceptions: Decomposing an improper fraction without dividing first
Using the wrong partial-fraction form for a repeated or quadratic factor

nodes.title: Integrate a product by parts
nodes.brief: Use the integration-by-parts formula: integral of first x (integral of second) minus integral of (derivative of first x integral of second).
nodes.quote: If we take f as the first function and g as the second function
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The formula: integral of (first)(second) = first*(integral of second) - integral of [(deriv of first)(integral of second)].
For a product, pick a FIRST function (to differentiate) and a SECOND (to integrate). Then integral of u*v dx = u*(integral of v) - integral of [(du/dx)*(integral of v)] dx. Example: integral of x cos x dx = x sin x - integral of sin x dx = x sin x + cos x + C.

PART 2 — Choose the first function by ILATE.
Choose the first function (to differentiate) by the ILATE order: Inverse-trig, Log, Algebraic, Trig, Exponential. Picking the wrong first function can make the integral harder. Integration by parts does not work for every product — it is for products where one factor simplifies on differentiating.
keyMoves: Choose a first function (to differentiate) and a second (to integrate)
Apply: integral = (first)(integral of second) - integral of [(derivative of first)(integral of second)]
Integrate the remaining (often simpler) integral
misconceptions: Choosing the first and second functions in a way that complicates the integral
Assuming integration by parts works for every product of functions

nodes.title: Define the definite integral
nodes.brief: Recognise the definite integral as a unique number, with lower limit a and upper limit b.
nodes.quote: The definite integral is introduced either as the limit of a sum or if it has an anti derivative F in the interval [a, b], then its value is the difference between the values of F at the end points, i.e., F(b) - F(a).
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The definite integral is a single NUMBER, with limits a and b.
The definite integral of f from a to b has a unique value (a number, not a family). Here a is the lower limit and b the upper limit. It is introduced as the limit of a sum (connecting it to area under the curve) or, when f has an antiderivative F, as F(b) - F(a).

PART 2 — No + C; mind the order of limits.
Unlike the indefinite integral, a definite integral takes NO constant of integration (it is a number). Swapping the limits negates the value: the integral from b to a is the negative of the integral from a to b.
keyMoves: Identify the lower limit a and upper limit b
Recognise the definite integral as a single number (an area)
Anticipate evaluating it as F(b) - F(a) using an antiderivative
misconceptions: Adding a constant of integration to a definite integral
Swapping the lower and upper limits without changing sign

nodes.title: Use the first Fundamental Theorem (A'(x) = f(x))
nodes.brief: Apply Theorem 1: the derivative of the area function equals the integrand.
nodes.quote: Then A'(x) = f(x), for all  $x \in [a, b]$ .
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The area function A(x) = integral of f from a to x.
For f continuous on [a, b], define the area-so-far function A(x) as the definite integral of f from the fixed lower limit a up to a variable upper limit x. A(x) measures the accumulated area under f from a to x.

PART 2 — Differentiating the area function gives back f: A'(x) = f(x).
The First Fundamental Theorem says A'(x) = f(x) for all x in [a, b]. So differentiation undoes the accumulation of area — integration and differentiation are inverse. Note A'(x) is f(x) (the integrand at x), NOT f(a) or a constant; this is what links area to antiderivatives.
keyMoves: Form the area function A(x) = integral of f from a to x
Differentiate it: A'(x) = f(x)
Read this as differentiation undoing integration
misconceptions: Thinking A'(x) gives f(a) or a constant rather than f(x)
Dropping the continuity hypothesis on f

nodes.title: Apply the King's-rule symmetry properties of the definite integral
nodes.brief: Two substitution-based properties (P3 and P4 in NCERT) let you replace x with a+b−x or a−x under the integral — essential for board-exam integrals that resist direct antidifferentiation.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — P3: replace x by a+b−x on [a, b].
Let t = a+b−x. Then dt = −dx; when x = a, t = b and when x = b, t = a. The reversed limits and the −dx flip cancel, giving ∫_a^b f(x) dx = ∫_a^b f(a+b−x) dx (NCERT Property P3). This lets you swap x with a+b−x inside the integrand without changing the value.

PART 2 — P4 (the special case a = 0): replace x by a−x on [0, a].
Setting b = 0 in P3 gives ∫_0^a f(x) dx = ∫_0^a f(a−x) dx (NCERT Property P4). The substitution t = a−x again reverses the limits and the sign, and the two negatives cancel. This is used most often to split trigonometric integrals over [0, π/2] or [0, π].

PART 3 — The standard trick: add I to itself after applying P3/P4.
Let I = ∫_a^b x f(x) dx. By P3 (assuming f(a+b−x) = f(x)): I = ∫_a^b (a+b−x) f(x) dx. Adding the two expressions gives 2I = (a+b) ∫_a^b f(x) dx, so I = (a+b)/2 · ∫_a^b f(x) dx. The same 'add I to itself' move underlies almost every board-exam use of this property.
keyMoves: State P3: ∫_a^b f(x) dx = ∫_a^b f(a+b−x) dx
State P4: ∫_0^a f(x) dx = ∫_0^a f(a−x) dx (special case)
Apply the 'add I to itself' trick to simplify ∫ x f(x) dx or trig integrals using the condition f(a+b−x)=f(x)
misconceptions: Thinking P3 changes the value — it gives an EQUAL integral, just with a rewritten integrand
Applying P4 to limits [a, b] with a ≠ 0 (P4 requires the lower limit to be 0)
Forgetting to add I to itself and equate; stopping after one application of P3/P4 gives 0 = 0

nodes.title: Evaluate a definite integral via the Fundamental Theorem (F(b) - F(a))
nodes.brief: Apply Theorem 2: the definite integral equals F(b) - F(a) for any antiderivative F of f.
nodes.quote: Let f be continuous function defined on the closed interval [a, b] and F be
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Find any antiderivative F of the integrand (no + C needed).
By the Second Fundamental Theorem, if f is continuous on [a, b] and F is any antiderivative of f (meaning F'(x) = f(x)), then the definite integral of f from a to b equals F(b) - F(a). Find F using whichever technique applies — standard integrals, substitution, integration by parts, or partial fractions — and omit the constant of integration since it cancels.

PART 2 — Evaluate F(b) - F(a), upper limit minus lower limit.
Substitute the upper limit b into F, then subtract F evaluated at the lower limit a. Example: definite integral of x^2 from 1 to 3 has antiderivative F(x) = x^3/3; so the answer is F(3) - F(1) = 27/3 - 1/3 = 26/3. The order is F(b) - F(a), NOT F(a) - F(b) — reversing the subtraction gives the wrong sign.
keyMoves: Find any one antiderivative F of the integrand f (constant C not needed)
Evaluate F at the upper limit b and the lower limit a
Compute the definite integral as F(b) - F(a)
misconceptions: Computing F(a) - F(b) instead of F(b) - F(a)
Carrying a constant of integration into a definite integral
